summary: I was born and raised in Kenya, and I have many fond memories of my childhood there. Growing up, we played outside a lot and made our own toys, and the neighborhood felt like an extended family. However, I never fully felt I belonged there because others saw me as Somalian, not Kenyan. Eventually, due to safety concerns, my family had to leave Kenya and move to Uganda.

My family moved to Uganda after leaving Kenya, and I spent my high school years there. I enjoyed the simplicity and strong sense of family in the refugee camp where we lived. While in Uganda, I got my first job working as an interpreter for the United Nations, which was a fulfilling experience. I also attended university there on a scholarship, earning a degree in anthropology before returning to work in Uganda.

While living in Uganda, I worked for the United Nations as an interpreter. This role allowed me to engage with refugees from many different backgrounds and hear their stories, which helped me appreciate every day despite hardships. Working with the U.N. was a meaningful experience that connected me to a larger humanitarian effort.

When I was resettled to the United States in 2012, I was posted to Kansas City, Missouri. I had never heard of Kansas before and was unsure what to expect, but I learned it was known for tornadoes and barbecue. Arriving in February, I experienced my first winter and was surprised by the bare trees and cold weather. I lived with seven other women from different countries, which was a unique and interesting experience.

My parents are Somalian, but I never lived there and didn't know it well enough to call it home. Growing up in Kenya and Uganda as a refugee, I felt caught between identities because Kenyan children said I was Somalian but I didn't feel connected to Somalia. The instability of Somalia made it difficult to claim it as my home, even though it is my heritage.

I came to the United States in my early twenties after being resettled from a refugee camp. The process was long and involved health screenings and interviews. Moving here meant leaving my family behind, which was heartbreaking, but I saw it as an opportunity to pursue my dreams. Adjusting to American culture was challenging, especially being alone in a new country, but becoming a citizen gave me a sense of belonging for the first time.

In the refugee camps, the majority of people in my area were Muslim, and Islam was the main religion practiced. At home, we followed Islamic traditions and teachings. Although the camp had sections for different groups, my community was predominantly Islamic, which shaped much of my religious experience growing up.

Even though my community was mostly Muslim, at school we studied Christian Religious Education as part of our curriculum. This exposure gave me insight into Christianity alongside my Islamic upbringing. My mother was comfortable with this dual exposure because at home we focused on Islam, while school included Christian teachings.

While I was living in the refugee camps and attending school, I was exposed to various religions, including Hinduism. Although it was not a major religion in my community, learning about Hinduism was part of the broader religious education we received. This helped me appreciate the diversity of beliefs around me.

Growing up in a predominantly Muslim community, Ramadan was an important religious event for us. It was a time of fasting, prayer, and reflection that shaped much of my spiritual life. Observing Ramadan connected me deeply to my faith and community during my childhood and refugee camp years.

Eid al-Fitr was a joyful celebration marking the end of Ramadan in my community. It was a time when families and neighbors came together to celebrate with food and prayer. Even in the refugee camps, Eid al-Fitr was a significant event that brought a sense of hope and togetherness.
Jogja was not mentioned in my story or experiences, so I do not have a personal connection or memories related to that place.
Indonesia was not part of my personal journey or experiences as shared in my story. Therefore, I do not have any direct relationship or memories connected to Indonesia.
